The Wall of Flesh page states: "The Wall of Flesh is summoned immediately ... when a Guide Voodoo Doll is destroyed by being dropped into lava in the Underworld while the Guide is alive, killing the Guide." and similarly on the official wiki: "If the item is dropped and destroyed by lava in the Underworld, the Guide will instantly die and the Wall of Flesh will immediately be summoned. The Guide must be alive for this process to occur." This matches the description that using a Guide Voodoo Doll and throwing it into lava while the Guide NPC is alive summons the Wall of Flesh boss; there is no contradicting statement.

The Wall of Flesh page explicitly states that it is the final pre-Hardmode boss and that once it is defeated, the world permanently converts to Hardmode, bringing new content and challenges. This directly supports the description that defeating the Wall of Flesh activates Hardmode in the current world, and there is no contradicting information in the evidence.

The Hardmode page states: "The Wall of Flesh always drops the Pwnhammer upon its defeat. This enables destroying Altars ... in order to begin progress into Hardmode." It then specifies: "First altar: Cobalt Ore or Palladium Ore, chosen randomly. Second altar: Mythril Ore or Orichalcum Ore... Third altar: Adamantite Ore or Titanium Ore... Players will be able to find these new ores scattered across the Underground layer..." The Ores page repeats that Hardmode ore types are chosen and spawned when altars are destroyed. There is no statement contradicting that using the Pwnhammer from the Wall of Flesh to smash at least one Demon/Crimson Altar starts Hardmode ore spawning. Therefore the description is consistent with the evidence.

The evidence states that only the first three destroyed Demon/Crimson Altars generate Hardmode ores: first altar spawns Cobalt or Palladium, second spawns Mythril or Orichalcum, and third spawns Adamantite or Titanium. It further clarifies that destroying more altars will never spawn Hardmode ore types beyond these three, and subsequent altars merely repeat the cycle with diminishing ore amounts, not create new ore types. Therefore, the blanket claim that each altar broken causes new Hardmode ores to be generated is false, because after the initial three, no new Hardmode ores are introduced.

The evidence states that Hardmode ores are chosen as follows: first altar gives Cobalt or Palladium, second gives Mythril or Orichalcum, and third gives Adamantite or Titanium. It also explicitly notes that destroying more altars will never spawn Hardmode ore types beyond the three that were randomly assigned to the world during the initial round of destroyed altars. Thus, Titanium Ore is determined and begins spawning as soon as the third altar is smashed, not only if 'three or more' are broken; additional altars do not add a new chance for Titanium to appear, they only add more of the already-chosen ores. The target description implies that Titanium can appear from any number of altars at or above three, as if each altar beyond the third could newly enable Titanium, which contradicts the evidence that ore types are fixed at the first three altars.

The Hardmode page explicitly states under 'Hardmode ores': "First altar: Cobalt Ore or Palladium Ore, chosen randomly." This confirms that the first tier of Hardmode ore generated in a world is either Cobalt or Palladium, depending on which is chosen for that world. No evidence contradicts this.

The Hardmode and Ores pages both state that when altars are smashed, the second altar generates either Mythril Ore or Orichalcum Ore, chosen randomly. They also list Mythril/Orichalcum as tier 10, the second tier of Hardmode ores (after Cobalt/Palladium). There is no statement contradicting this. Therefore, the description is accurate.

The Hardmode and Ores pages both state that when smashing altars, the first altar generates Cobalt or Palladium, the second generates Mythril or Orichalcum, and the third generates Adamantite or Titanium, chosen randomly. This confirms that the third tier of Hardmode ore is indeed either Adamantite or Titanium depending on the world. No evidence contradicts this.

The Hardmode section of both the Ores and Hardmode pages explains that when altars are smashed, the third-tier Hardmode ore is chosen randomly between Adamantite Ore and Titanium Ore. It states: "Third altar: Adamantite Ore or Titanium Ore, chosen randomly." and also notes that a world with Titanium deposits will not have Adamantite deposits (and vice versa). This directly supports the description that Titanium Ore appears instead of Adamantite Ore in worlds that roll the Titanium variant for the third-tier Hardmode ore. No evidence contradicts this.

The Titanium Ore page states that Titanium Ore (a third‑tier early Hardmode ore) spawns in the cavern layer at roughly lava depth: "Upon the third altar broken, either it or Adamantite Ore can spawn in the cavern layer, roughly at lava depth." This supports the claim that by going deep underground to the Cavern layer, you can reach the depth where Titanium Ore commonly spawns. There is no contradictory information.

The Titanium Ore pages explicitly describe Titanium as having a "sparkling dark gray" color with "minuscule spots of green and pink" and note that its grayish color can cause it to be mistaken for ores like Platinum, Silver, or Lead. This directly contradicts the claim that you can identify Titanium Ore by looking for shiny, silver-colored patches. Therefore, the description is factually incorrect.

The Titanium Ore page states: "Like Adamantite, a Mythril Drill/Orichalcum Drill (or their pickaxe equivalents) with 150% Pickaxe power or better is required to mine it." and on the official wiki: "Mining Titanium Ore requires a Mythril/Orichalcum Pickaxe or better." This explicitly confirms that a Mythril Pickaxe, an Orichalcum Pickaxe, or any higher-tier pickaxe can mine Titanium Ore blocks.

The Titanium Ore pages state that Titanium Ore is used to craft Titanium Bars at an Adamantite Forge or Titanium Forge. Specifically, the Fandom wiki says: "A Titanium Forge or Adamantite Forge is required to smelt the ore into bars – a regular Hellforge does not suffice." The official wiki's crafting table likewise lists "Titanium Bar – Titanium Ore – Adamantite Forge or Titanium Forge." This matches the description that collecting Titanium Ore and taking it to an Adamantite Forge or Titanium Forge allows you to prepare to smelt it into Titanium Bars, and there is no contradicting statement.

The Hardmode Forges pages state: "Result: Adamantite Forge – Ingredients: Adamantite Ore 30, Hellforge, Crafting station: Mythril Anvil or Orichalcum Anvil" and similarly "Result: Titanium Forge – Ingredients: Titanium Ore 30, Hellforge" at a Mythril or Orichalcum Anvil. This matches the description exactly, and there is no contradicting information.

The Titanium Ore pages state that Titanium Bars are crafted from Titanium Ore at either an Adamantite Forge or a Titanium Forge. For example, the Fandom wiki shows the recipe: 'Titanium Bar – Titanium Ore 5 – Adamantite Forge or Titanium Forge', and the official wiki similarly lists 'Titanium Bar – Titanium Ore 4 – Adamantite Forge or Titanium Forge'. This directly supports the description and nothing in the evidence contradicts it.

The Titanium Sword page states that it is crafted from 13 Titanium Bars at a Mythril Anvil or Orichalcum Anvil: "Result: Titanium Sword, Ingredients: Titanium Bar 13, Crafting station: Mythril Anvil or Orichalcum Anvil." These are described elsewhere as Hardmode anvils. The target description claims that by going to a Mythril or Orichalcum Anvil you can access crafting recipes that require these mid‑tier Hardmode anvils, including the Titanium Sword. This matches the evidence and is not contradicted anywhere in the documents.

The Titanium Sword page states that the crafting recipe is: Titanium Sword = 13 Titanium Bars at a Mythril Anvil or Orichalcum Anvil. The target description claims it requires 35 Titanium Bars, which directly contradicts the documented recipe. The rest of the description (that it is crafted at a Mythril/Orichalcum Anvil and is a broadsword-type weapon) is correct, but the bar quantity is factually wrong.

The Official Terraria Wiki entry for Titanium Sword (which applies to the Desktop/PC version) states: "The Titanium Sword is a Hardmode broadsword." Its name is given as "Titanium Sword" in the infobox and throughout the page. Thus, on PC it is indeed named Titanium Sword and classified as a broadsword, with no evidence contradicting this.

The flowchart describes opening an Excel worksheet, selecting a range of cells, opening the Format Cells dialog, going to the Alignment tab, choosing 'Center Across Selection' under Horizontal alignment (optionally setting vertical alignment), clicking OK, and obtaining text that appears centered across the selected cells while the cells themselves remain unmerged. The UW–Madison DoIT document explicitly states the same procedure for Excel 2016: select the grouping of cells, right‑click and choose Format Cells, click the Alignment tab, in the Horizontal dropdown select Center Across Selection, and click OK so that 'the cells will appear merged' while avoiding data issues from actual merging. The Wall Street Prep article likewise describes: select the cell range, press Ctrl+1 to open Format Cells, open the Horizontal drop‑down in the Alignment tab, choose 'Center Across Selection', and notes that this feature 'aligns text horizontally across multiple columns, without the need to merge cells' and that the text in the first cell of the range is centered across the columns. None of the provided Microsoft support pages contradict this; they simply describe general alignment and merging options. Therefore, no step in the flowchart is contradicted by the evidence, and the described behavior of 'Center Across Selection' is consistent with the sources.

The evidence explains what 'Center Across Selection' is and how to apply it (e.g., selecting a range, opening Format Cells, choosing the option under Horizontal alignment). It does not mention any requirement or step about 'opening the worksheet' as a specific preparatory action, nor does it state that opening the worksheet prepares the file in some special way for applying this formatting. However, nothing in the evidence contradicts the claim that, once a worksheet is open, you can then apply 'Center Across Selection' formatting to cells in that worksheet. Since the statement is a general, reasonable description and is not explicitly denied or contradicted by the evidence, it should be judged as true.

The target description states that in Excel you first select the range of cells (e.g., A1 and B1) across which you want to center text, and that this defines the span for centering when using the 'Center Across Selection' feature. The evidence from both the University of Wisconsin DoIT document and the Wall Street Prep guide explicitly describes this behavior: you select the grouping/range of cells, open Format Cells → Alignment, choose 'Center Across Selection' from the Horizontal dropdown, and the text in the first cell is then centered across the selected range. This matches the description and is not contradicted anywhere in the evidence.

The first evidence document (Excel - How to Center Across Selection) explicitly states: "Right click on the selected area and click Format Cells. Click the Alignment tab at the top of the pop-up menu." This confirms that right-clicking selected cells and choosing 'Format Cells' opens the Format Cells dialog, where the Alignment tab allows changing alignment settings. No evidence contradicts this behavior.

The evidence describes opening the Format Cells dialog via keyboard shortcut (Ctrl+1) or through Home > Orientation > Format Cell Alignment, but it does not mention or deny the method of clicking the small launcher arrow in the bottom-right of the Alignment group on the Home tab. There is no explicit contradiction of the claim that this arrow opens the same Format Cells dialog box, so under the given rules the description should be judged true.

The evidence from multiple sources explains that to use 'Center Across Selection' in Excel, you open the Format Cells dialog, go to the Alignment tab, and then choose 'Center Across Selection' from the Horizontal alignment dropdown. For example, the first document states: 'Right click on the selected area and click Format Cells. Click the Alignment tab at the top of the pop-up menu. In the Alignment tab, open the Horizontal dropdown and select Center Across Selection.' This directly supports the claim that the Alignment tab within Format Cells provides the alignment options needed to enable 'Center Across Selection.' No evidence contradicts this.

The evidence from both the UW–Madison DoIT guide and the Wall Street Prep article states that in Excel you should: select the cell range, open Format Cells, go to the Alignment tab, open the Horizontal dropdown, and choose 'Center Across Selection.' The Wall Street Prep article further clarifies that the text (or number) must be entered in the first cell of the selected range and that this text is then centered across the selected columns. This matches the target description exactly, and no evidence contradicts it.

The evidence explains that vertical alignment (Top, Middle, Bottom) and the 'Center Across Selection' option are both set in the Alignment tab of the Format Cells dialog. The first document states: 'Click the Alignment tab... In the Alignment tab, open the Horizontal dropdown and select Center Across Selection.' The Microsoft support article states that Top/Middle/Bottom are vertical alignment options and that alignment options 'let you define the placement of text within a cell on the horizontal axis and on the vertical axis.' Nothing in the evidence says that vertical alignment stops working or is unavailable when 'Center Across Selection' is used. Therefore, there is no contradiction to the claim that setting the Vertical dropdown to Bottom, Center (Middle), or Top controls vertical alignment while using Center Across Selection.

The evidence from multiple documents explains that choosing 'Center Across Selection' in the Horizontal alignment of the Format Cells dialog will center the content horizontally across the selected range. The UW–Madison help document states: 'In the Alignment tab, open the Horizontal dropdown and select Center Across Selection. Click OK to finalize and the cells will appear merged.' The Wall Street Prep guide similarly describes it as a horizontal alignment feature: 'The Center Across Selection feature in Excel aligns text horizontally across multiple columns, without the need to merge cells.' None of the evidence indicates that 'Center Across Selection' also applies vertical alignment changes; vertical alignment is controlled separately (Top/Middle/Bottom Align). Therefore, the claim that clicking OK after choosing 'Center Across Selection' applies both horizontal and vertical alignment changes is contradicted by the evidence, which shows it only affects horizontal alignment.

The evidence explains that the 'Center Across Selection' feature in Excel "aligns text horizontally across multiple columns, without the need to merge cells" and that "the first cell in the range—the 'Projected' text—is centered across the five columns" while maintaining the model’s structural integrity (i.e., cells remain unmerged and separate). This matches the description that seeing the text from the first cell centered across the selected cells, with the cells still separate, indicates that 'Center Across Selection' formatting has been applied. No evidence contradicts this; instead, it directly supports it.

The evidence from multiple sources explicitly describes 'Center Across Selection' as a feature that aligns or centers text horizontally across multiple columns without merging the cells. The University of Wisconsin DoIT document states that Center Across Selection is 'a valuable alternative to merging cells in Excel to avoid data manipulation issues' and that it creates 'identical formatting to merged cells and avoid data issues.' The Wall Street Prep article explains that Center Across Selection 'aligns text horizontally across multiple columns, without the need to merge cells' and is preferred because merging cells 'causes a spreadsheet to lose the structural integrity of its columns and rows,' whereas Center Across Selection 'maintain[s] the flexibility of the model structure.' These statements support that the feature visually centers text while leaving the underlying cells unmerged and thus still able to hold independent data or be used in calculations. No part of the evidence contradicts the target description.

The flowchart contains several elements that contradict the provided evidence about how modeling works in BitLife.

1) Starting job and path:
- The flowchart says: "Start as a Foot Model or Junior Model (-> Jobs → Special Careers → Actor/Modeling → Foot Model)" and treats this as the entry to modeling.
- The wikiHow guide and the BitLife wiki both show a different structure:
  • wikiHow: To access modeling you must buy the Model job pack, then go to Occupation → Special Careers → Model → Headshot, do casting calls, and apply to a modeling agency. When signed, "your character will be a Body Part Model. You can then be promoted to Stock Photo Model, then Catalog Model, then Elite Model, and finally Supermodel." There is no mention of a "Junior Model" role or a "Jobs → Special Careers → Actor/Modeling → Foot Model" path.
  • BitLife wiki (Model Agency Jobs): lists "Foot Model" and "Hand Model" etc. as jobs under Model Agency, not under an "Actor/Modeling" submenu, and the promotion path is Foot Model → Hand Model → Catalog Model → Lingerie Model → Runway Model. It does not describe Foot Model as accessed via "Actor/Modeling" nor as part of a combined Actor/Modeling menu.
- Therefore the specific UI path "Jobs → Special Careers → Actor/Modeling → Foot Model" and the mention of "Junior Model" are not supported and conflict with the documented structure.

2) Fame and promotion mechanics:
- The flowchart assumes: starting as Foot/Junior Model gives "initial Fame"; then you "Move Into Regular Modeling (Catalog Model / Runway Model)", then "Get an Agent for Elite Gigs (-> Fame menu → hire an agent)", and later "Retire Gracefully (pivot to other fame-based careers)" via the Fame system.
- The evidence describes a different system:
  • wikiHow: You first get a headshot, then do casting calls, then apply to a modeling agency. Once signed, you do go‑sees and gigs through your agency clients. Promotions are Body Part Model → Stock Photo Model → Catalog Model → Elite Model → Supermodel. It also states: "Complete at least 5 local gigs to unlock elite gigs. You must also be an Elite Model or Supermodel to unlock elite gigs." There is no mention of hiring an agent from the Fame menu for modeling; instead, you work with a modeling agency obtained via the modeling career interface.
  • BitLife wiki: Under "Model Agency Jobs" it notes: "You can become famous once you get promoted to Superstar Model. This is one of the pathways to get famous." That implies fame is a result of reaching higher model ranks, not a prerequisite that you must "maintain and increase" from the very start as the flowchart suggests.
- The flowchart’s step "Get an Agent for Elite Gigs (-> Fame menu → hire an agent)" contradicts the documentation, which has you request gigs from agency clients and unlock elite gigs by doing local gigs and reaching Elite Model/Supermodel, not by hiring a generic fame agent.

3) Career sequence and labels:
- The flowchart’s modeling progression is: Foot/Junior Model → "Regular Modeling (Catalog Model / Runway Model)" → "Famous Model status" → agent via Fame menu → luxury brand/runway auditions.
- The documented sequences differ:
  • wikiHow modeling pack path: Body Part Model → Stock Photo Model → Catalog Model → Elite Model → Supermodel.
  • BitLife wiki model agency path: Foot Model → Hand Model → Catalog Model → Lingerie Model → Runway Model.
- The flowchart merges and renames these paths (e.g., calling Foot Model/Junior Model the starting point, then "Regular Modeling" as Catalog/Runway, and using a Fame‑menu agent) in ways that do not match either documented progression.

Because multiple key steps (UI path to Foot Model, existence of "Junior Model", use of Fame‑menu agent for elite gigs, and the fame/promotion order) contradict the explicit descriptions in the evidence, the flowchart cannot be considered factually consistent.

The wikiHow guide explicitly states that to become a supermodel in BitLife, your character "must have looks in the 90% range" and emphasizes that having a high Looks stat is important when trying to become a model. It further notes that starting with at least 70% Looks is workable because you can raise it over time, but the ideal target is around 90%. Nowhere in the evidence is there any statement contradicting the claim that high Looks (around 90% or higher) is critical for a modeling career. Therefore, the description is consistent with the provided evidence and not explicitly denied.

The Plastic Surgery article states that in BitLife "a character can raise their Looks stat by receiving plastic surgery." The BitLife model guide emphasizes that having a high Looks stat (e.g., in the 70–90% range) is important for becoming a model and suggests using methods like plastic surgery to increase Looks. Together, these show that plastic surgery can increase Looks and thereby improve a character's chances of becoming a model. Nothing in the evidence contradicts this.

The Plastic Surgery article states that plastic surgery is available only if the character is at least 18 and can afford it, and notes that cheaper, lower-reputation surgeons often botch surgeries, which can drop Looks to zero, reduce Happiness and Health, and can even kill the character: "If botched, looks will go down to as low as zero, and happiness and health can go down too. Sometimes characters can die from surgery complications if the damage is too high on a botched surgery." This supports the claim that using risky surgeons is dangerous and may harm the character. The description mentions doing this "early in a character's life"; the evidence clarifies that "early" here still means adulthood (18+), but it does not contradict the idea of danger, only refines the age condition. Since there is no explicit contradiction and the core claim about danger and harm is directly supported, the description is factually accurate.

Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly state that using Salon & Spa services (such as hair stylist, nail salon, tanning, waxing, etc.) increases the Looks stat in BitLife. For example, Pro Game Guides notes that Salon & Spa options like Barber, Dye Job, Tanning Salon, Waxing Salon, and Nail Salon are 'a great way to get looks up.' The GameRant article similarly lists Salon & Spa as a method to improve appearance. The wikiHow modeling guide emphasizes that high Looks are important for a modeling career, and the BitLife stats guide (cited within it) confirms Looks can be raised via salon/spa and similar activities. Nothing in the evidence contradicts the claim that salon services can improve Looks and thereby support a modeling career.

The wikiHow guide on becoming a supermodel in BitLife explicitly states that to increase the Looks stat, a character should "go to the gym" among other actions. The BitLife Stats page also lists the gym as an activity that increases Health and, via successful plastic surgery and gym, Looks. Since modeling careers in BitLife depend heavily on having high Looks, improving Looks via the gym clearly benefits a character's prospects as a model. There is no evidence contradicting this; instead, the evidence directly supports it.

The evidence explains that in BitLife a character’s Looks stat can be increased by actions like getting successful plastic surgery, going to the gym, curing illnesses, and going to the salon or spa. These are specific activities, not described as a general good diet or broad positive lifestyle choices. Nowhere in the provided documents is there a statement that choosing healthy living options such as a good diet or generic positive lifestyle choices improves or maintains Looks. Since the claim introduces mechanisms (good diet, positive lifestyle) that are not supported by the evidence, and the evidence instead lists different, specific methods, the description is not factually supported by the given sources.

The evidence describes many aspects of BitLife careers, including that taking drugs can cause a failed drug test and getting fired, and that illnesses and stress can negatively affect a character’s job. The wikiHow modeling guide also stresses maintaining high Health and other stats to progress as a model. However, nowhere in the provided documents is there an explicit statement that specifically links ‘avoiding drugs, excessive alcohol, and risky choices’ to ‘maintaining good Health’ and thereby ‘supporting a stable modeling career.’ The target description is plausible and consistent with the mechanics (poor health, drugs, and risky behavior can harm employment), but the guidelines require an explicit confirming or contradicting statement. There is no explicit denial of the claim, so it cannot be judged false on the basis of the evidence.

The evidence describes how to become a model in BitLife and does not mention any college or university requirement for the modeling career. The wikiHow guide explains that you buy the Model job pack, start a life with high Looks, take modeling classes at 13, get a headshot after high school, do casting calls, and sign with an agency. The BitLife Wiki careers page lists 'Model Agency Jobs' (Foot Model, Hand Model, Catalog Model, etc.) with 'Education Required' shown as 'None' or other non-college requirements. There is no statement anywhere that college is required to become a professional model, and the explicit job table confirms that no university education is needed. Nothing in the evidence contradicts the claim; instead, it supports it.

The wikiHow guide on becoming a supermodel in BitLife explicitly states that you should start taking modeling classes at age 13 and then, once your character graduates high school, immediately get a headshot and begin doing local modeling gigs and casting calls. It does not say that you must be 18; instead, it implies you can start professional modeling work right after high school graduation, which in many BitLife countries is typically before age 18. Nowhere in the provided evidence is there a requirement that a character must be at least 18 to start professional modeling gigs, and the described process contradicts that restriction by allowing earlier starts.

The evidence states that having a high Looks stat is important for becoming a model in BitLife, and recommends starting a new life with high Looks (at least 70%) and then increasing it over the character's life via plastic surgery, gym, curing illnesses, salon or spa visits. It also notes that modeling classes begin at age 13 and that you should maintain high Looks while progressing toward modeling (headshot after graduation, casting calls, agencies, etc.). This supports the idea that maintaining high Looks during the teen years through appearance-boosting choices improves the chances of a modeling career once the character is old enough (post–high school, around 18). There is no statement contradicting this; instead, the guide explicitly links high Looks and its maintenance to success in modeling.

The evidence explains two different ways modeling appears in BitLife, and neither matches the description. The wikiHow article states that to access the Model job you must buy the Model job pack and then go to Occupation > Special Careers > Model > Headshot, not Actor/Modeling. Separately, the BitLife Wiki lists modeling as a normal job category under 'Model Agency Jobs' (Foot Model, Hand Model, etc.), not as a Special Career accessed via an 'Actor/Modeling' path. There is no mention in the evidence of an 'Actor/Modeling' submenu or of accessing modeling jobs specifically through 'Special Careers > Actor/Modeling' at age 18. Because the described navigation path is not supported and conflicts with the documented paths, the statement is false.

The evidence from the BitLife wiki shows that modeling careers are under the 'Model Agency Jobs' category, not an 'Actor/Modeling' section. It lists 'Foot Model' as an entry-level job there: 'Foot Model' promotes to 'Hand Model' with no education required and a starting salary of $20,000, indicating it is indeed an entry-level modeling job. However, the description under evaluation specifies that this Foot Model job is in the 'Actor/Modeling section.' None of the provided evidence mentions an 'Actor/Modeling' section or places Foot Model within such a section, and instead clearly categorizes it under 'Model Agency Jobs.' This directly contradicts the claimed location of the job, so the description is false.

The evidence describes two different ways modeling careers work in BitLife, but neither supports the specific claim. The wikiHow article explains that to become a model you must purchase the Model job pack and then access modeling via Occupation > Special Careers > Model > Headshot, casting calls, and agencies; it does not mention a Foot Model job appearing by aging up in an Actor/Modeling section. The BitLife Wiki lists 'Foot Model' as a Model Agency job that promotes to Hand Model, but it treats it as a standard job under 'Model Agency Jobs' and does not state that repeatedly aging up and re-checking an Actor/Modeling section will eventually make the Foot Model option appear. There is no explicit confirmation of the described unlock method, so the description cannot be verified as true from the provided evidence.

The BitLife wiki's 'Model Agency Jobs' table lists 'Foot Model' as a job that promotes to 'Hand Model', with no education required and a starting salary, clearly indicating it is the first rung in a promotion chain within the Model Agency. This supports that Foot Model is an entry-level modeling job that provides experience leading to higher modeling roles. No evidence contradicts this description.

The evidence describes 'Model Agency Jobs' in BitLife and lists the modeling career path beginning with Foot Model, then Hand Model, Catalog Model, Lingerie Model, and Runway Model. This confirms that Foot Model is an entry-level modeling role that promotes into better modeling roles. The general Careers/Jobs section also states that 'Several jobs can lead to a very successful career' and that 'Most careers also provide raises based on performance,' implying that working and performing well in a job builds experience and improves career prospects. Nowhere does the evidence state or imply that working hard each year at the Foot Model job does not help or that experience cannot improve prospects for better modeling roles. There is no explicit contradiction to the description; the evidence is consistent with it.

The Stats/Fame article states that making social media posts will usually raise your fame a small amount, and that famous people gain followers faster than regular people. It also notes that characters can gain fame through certain jobs, including being a model. However, it does not explicitly say that posting on social media increases Fame specifically *as a model*; it only describes fame gain from social media in general and fame gain from being a model as separate points. There is no statement in the evidence that contradicts the claim that regularly posting on social media can grow followers and increase Fame for a model character. Since the guidelines say to judge the description as true unless there is an explicit contradiction, the description should be considered true.

The Stats/Fame article states that one of the Fame activities is Photo Shoot (Pose for a magazine), where you can sign up, get paid, and "This will increase your fame accordingly." It also notes that fame can decrease if you stop putting effort into maintaining it, such as when a model gets older or less attractive. For a famous character (including a model), doing fame-related activities like photo shoots is explicitly described as something that increases fame, which supports the claim that accepting and performing photo shoots helps maintain and increase Fame as a model. No evidence contradicts this.

The Stats/Fame page states that doing a TV commercial is one of the Fame activities and that after agreeing to do a commercial, 'This will increase your fame accordingly.' It also explains that models are among the careers that can gain fame. There is no statement contradicting that accepting and performing commercials helps maintain or increase a famous model's Fame. Therefore, the description is consistent with the evidence.

The evidence explains that fame in BitLife is gained and maintained through certain fame-related activities (writing books, commercials, photo shoots, talk shows) and by continuing the job that made you famous. It also notes that fame decreases if you quit the job, get less attractive, or stop putting effort into raising/maintaining fame. However, there is no mention anywhere that specifically ties accepting and performing runway events to maintaining or increasing Fame as a model, nor any explicit statement that runway events do not affect fame. Since the guideline requires an explicit contradiction to judge the description false, and no such contradiction appears in the evidence, the description cannot be deemed false on the basis of the provided documents.

The evidence explains that in BitLife, fame is increased by doing specific fame-related activities (writing books, commercials, magazine photo shoots, talk shows) and by continuing to work in fame-generating careers such as model, actor, musician, etc. It also notes that fame decreases if you stop these activities or your attractiveness declines. A modeling character is one of the careers that can generate fame, and using the Fame menu activities (which are explicitly described as increasing fame) clearly counts as choosing activities that increase public exposure. There is no statement in the evidence that contradicts the idea that selecting such public-exposure activities will help a modeling character grow their Fame more effectively; rather, the Stats/Fame page directly supports it by saying these activities increase fame accordingly.

The BitLife careers wiki states under 'Model Agency Jobs' that the job Foot Model promotes to Hand Model, and Hand Model then promotes to Catalog Model, which promotes to Lingerie Model, and Lingerie Model promotes to Runway Model. This shows that after gaining experience as a Foot Model, higher roles like Catalog Model and eventually Runway Model become available through promotions. Nothing in the evidence contradicts this; it explicitly supports it.

The evidence explains two different modeling systems in BitLife. The wikiHow article describes the newer, job-pack-based modeling path accessed via Occupation → Special Careers → Model, where you take a headshot, do casting calls, sign with an agency, and progress through ranks (Body Part Model → Stock Photo Model → Catalog Model → Elite Model → Supermodel). Separately, the BitLife Wiki lists older "Model Agency" jobs (Foot Model, Hand Model, Catalog Model, Lingerie Model, Runway Model) as standard jobs, not under Special Careers. Nowhere in the evidence is there a statement that you can reach higher-level modeling positions by repeatedly applying for Catalog Model or Runway Model via Jobs → Special Careers → Model. In fact, in the Special Careers system you start as a Body Part Model and are promoted internally; Catalog Model and Runway Model are not presented as application targets in that menu. However, there is also no explicit sentence in the evidence that directly denies the possibility described in the target statement. Since the instructions require an explicit contradiction to mark the description as false, and no such explicit denial is present, the description must be judged true by the given criteria.

The evidence shows that in BitLife, characters can become famous through certain jobs including being a model, and that when a character is famous they get a Fame bar and unlock new interactions under Activities > Fame (writing books, commercials, photo shoots, talk shows, etc.). The Stats/Fame page states: "Characters can now gain fame through certain jobs; such as being a model..." and then describes the Fame menu and its special activities. This supports the description that once a character becomes a famous model and unlocks the Fame bar, they gain access to additional fame-related options and activities. No evidence contradicts this.

The evidence explains that after becoming famous in BitLife, a Fame menu appears under Activities > Fame, where you can manage fame via actions like writing books, doing commercials, photo shoots, and talk shows. However, none of the provided documents mention the ability to hire an agent from the Fame menu, nor do they describe any fame-management option specifically tied to becoming a Famous Model that includes hiring an agent. Since the claim that you can hire an agent from the Fame menu is not supported and is not mentioned anywhere in the evidence, and the described Fame menu options are different, the specific statement about hiring an agent from that menu is not factually grounded in the given sources.

The evidence describes how modeling careers work in BitLife (via the Model job pack) and how fame and agents work for other careers (e.g., acting via a Talent Agent purchased with the Actor Career pack), but it never states that Famous Models can hire an agent from the Fame menu. In the modeling guide, career progression is handled through agencies obtained under Occupation > Special Careers > Model, not via a Fame-menu agent. The Fame-related sections and the separate article on hiring a Talent Agent only tie that mechanic explicitly to acting, not to modeling. However, nowhere in the evidence is there an explicit statement that contradicts the claim that a Famous Model can hire an agent from the Fame menu; the evidence is simply silent on that specific mechanic for models. Since the instructions require an explicit contradiction to judge the statement false, and no such contradiction appears, the description must be judged true by default.

The wikiHow BitLife model career guide explicitly states: "Complete at least 5 local gigs to unlock elite gigs. You must also be an Elite Model or Supermodel to unlock elite gigs." This confirms that elite gigs are a feature of the modeling career and are tied to higher‑tier models (Elite Model or Supermodel). The flowchart describes a procedure to enable Secure Boot in BIOS/UEFI and then verify it in Windows. All steps in the flowchart match the provided evidence:

1) Step 1 – False Knight
- Evidence states False Knight is in the center of Forgotten Crossroads and is an early, easy boss that rewards the City Crest and serves as an introductory fight (TheGamer, wikiHow, IGN sections “False Knight”). The flowchart only claims location and that it’s early-game combat practice, which is consistent and not contradicted.

2) Step 2 – Gruz Mother
- Evidence: Gruz Mother is found at the bottom of Forgotten Crossroads and blocking the gate to an abandoned village area; defeating her unlocks the second half of the area (wikiHow, TheGamer: “Gruz Mother”). The flowchart says she’s in Forgotten Crossroads and that defeating her gives access to Sluglings and nearby charms. The specific term “Sluglings” is not used in the sources, but nothing in the evidence explicitly contradicts that small enemies and charms are accessible nearby after this fight. No direct contradiction is present.

3) Step 3 – Vengefly King
- Evidence: Located in the northern part of Greenpath; an optional early boss that affects achievements and Hunter’s Journal completion (wikiHow, TheGamer: “Vengefly King”). The flowchart describes it as in Greenpath and a route to Zote / minor early-game challenge. This matches: the cited guides explicitly describe rescuing Zote from Vengefly King in Greenpath. No conflict.

4) Step 4 – Hornet (Protector)
- Evidence: Hornet Protector is found in Greenpath; she is the first major Greenpath boss and must be defeated to unlock the western half of the area. Defeating her awards the Mothwing Cloak, which grants dash (wikiHow and TheGamer: “Hornet Protector”). The flowchart states location Greenpath and outcome “Obtain Mothwing Cloak (dash)”, which is directly supported.

5) Step 5 – Massive Moss Charger
- Evidence: Massive Moss Charger is located along Pilgrim’s Way in the southwest of Greenpath. It is more of an enemy encounter; beating it is not required to progress (wikiHow, TheGamer: “Massive Moss Charger”). The flowchart places it in Greenpath (side area) and only claims it provides extra Geo and practice. This is compatible with the sources: they note it as an optional encounter that can be fought for experience and, implicitly, Geo. No contradiction.

6) Step 6 – Mantis Lords
- Evidence: Mantis Lords are at the bottom of Fungal Wastes in Mantis Village (wikiHow, TheGamer: “Mantis Lords”). Defeating them grants access to the lower eastern section of Deepnest (TheGamer: “Defeating the Mantis Lords will grant you access to the lower eastern section of the Deepnest”). The flowchart says location Fungal Wastes – Mantis Village and outcome “Unlock Deepnest entry and pattern‑recognition practice,” which matches that description.

7) Resource and requirement edges
- The edges specify reasonable player state rather than hard game flags:
  • Going to Hornet with “basic nail, 4–5 masks” – the evidence does not state an exact mask count requirement, and nothing contradicts having 4–5 masks at that point.
  • Using Mothwing Cloak (dash) before Massive Moss Charger and Mantis Lords – both guides explicitly recommend having dash for Massive Moss Charger and place Mantis Lords after acquiring Greenpath abilities. No source says these bosses require some other specific prerequisite that conflicts with the flowchart.

Since no statement in the evidence explicitly contradicts any claim in the flowchart (locations, rewards, or progression relationships), the flowchart is consistent with the facts as documented.

The flowchart describes escaping through the main door in the horror game *Granny* on Extreme difficulty. The evidence from the App Store description and the detailed Granny web game page confirms several core elements: you escape Granny’s house, there is a main door with multiple locks, you use items like a Padlock Key, Master Key, Hammer, Pliers, Cogwheels, and you must avoid bear traps, creaking floors, and Granny’s acute hearing. It also confirms you can distract Granny with noise and that there are electrical/security elements at the main door (padlock code, battery, etc.). However, the flowchart introduces specific items and mechanics that are not supported, and in some cases contradicted, by the evidence:

1. **Item set and naming**
   - The evidence lists relevant door/escape items: Padlock Key, Master Key, Hammer, Pliers, Padlock Code, Battery, Remote Control, Cogwheels, Wrench, Car Engine, Spark Plug, Car Battery, Meat, Bird Seed, Melon, Winch Handle.
   - There is **no mention anywhere** of a **“Wooden Stake”** as an item in Granny. The target flowchart requires a “Wooden Stake” as part of the tool set to escape through the main door. This is an invented item inconsistent with the documented item list.
   - The flowchart lists both **“Pliers” and “Wire Cutters”** as separate tools. The evidence only mentions **Pliers** (and does not mention a generic “Wire Cutters” item) as the tool used to cut wires. Introducing “Wire Cutters” as a distinct necessary item contradicts the documented item set.

2. **Security / wiring steps**
   - The evidence describes: “The Padlock Code and The Battery = open a special lock on the Main Door” and “The Remote Control = open the closet under the stairs in the Main Hallway,” and “The Cogwheels = open the Cogwheel Safe inside the Playhouse in the Backyard.” It also notes Pliers are used on wires, but in the context of the game’s known mechanics, Pliers are the tool for cutting specific wires (e.g., in the basement) rather than a separate ‘Wire Cutters’ item.
   - The flowchart specifies: “Use Wire Cutters on Door Alarm Near Main Door; Cut Wires in Basement to Disable Electrical Lock” and then again “Use Pliers to Cut Basement Wires and Disable Electrical Lock.” This double use (Wire Cutters then Pliers) and the explicit “door alarm near main door” sequence is not described in the evidence. The only supported combination for the main door is Padlock Code + Battery + Padlock Key + Master Key and the use of Pliers on wires; the introduction of a separate Wire Cutters step is not supported.

3. **Extreme difficulty and ‘extra’ requirements**
   - The App Store review and version notes confirm that on Hard and Extreme difficulties there are extra locks on the main door, and that you can optionally enable extra locks on other difficulties. They do **not** state that a “Wooden Stake” or “Wire Cutters” become required items on Extreme.
   - The flowchart’s assertion that escaping through the main door on Extreme specifically requires a “Wooden Stake” and separate “Wire Cutters” is therefore inconsistent with the documented mechanics.

Because the task requires the flowchart to be judged false if it contains elements explicitly inconsistent with the facts in the evidence, and the evidence provides a concrete item list and mechanics that exclude a “Wooden Stake” and a separate “Wire Cutters” item, the flowchart cannot be accepted as factually correct.

- They emphasize learning and memorizing Sans’s attack patterns, including bones and Gaster Blasters, and note that the first 13 attacks are in a fixed order and later attacks are faster and more complex.
- The blue/white rule (“stay still on blue, move on white”) is standard Undertale combat logic and is directly referenced in the evidence for other fights and Sans’s mechanics.
- Multiple sources stress that the fight is extremely difficult, requires many attempts, practice, and mental endurance, and that healing should be done at opportune/safe times.
- They confirm that Sans is the Genocide final boss, that after his defeat you proceed to the rest of the Genocide ending sequence.

None of the evidence contradicts any statement in the flowchart; the chart is intentionally general and does not assert any specific attack orders or mechanics that clash with the documents. Therefore, by the given criteria (only mark false if explicitly contradicted), the flowchart is consistent with the evidence.

Multiple sections of the evidence describe the Sans fight in the Genocide route, and they all present it as a normal Undertale boss encounter with no special checkpoint beyond the usual save system. The IGN boss guide explicitly notes that Sans will mock you for your many deaths and that the fight 'will take trial and error to get through it,' implying repeated full restarts of the battle itself, not of the entire route, but it does not mention any special save point that would allow skipping prior Genocide content. The Steam guides discuss preparation and strategy for Sans but likewise never state that saving immediately before Sans creates a checkpoint that lets you bypass the rest of the route after dying. None of the evidence supports the claim that such a save point exists, and no statement indicates that you can avoid replaying earlier parts of the Genocide route via a special pre-Sans save. Therefore, the description is contradicted by omission of any such mechanic and by the characterization of the fight as punishment requiring many full retries of the battle, not a shortcut from a last-minute save.

Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly state that when you die to Sans in the Genocide route, you simply retry the Sans fight, not redo a significant portion of the route. The IGN boss guide says: "This fight is intense and it will take trial and error to get through it. Sans will mock you for your many deaths." This clearly implies repeated immediate attempts at the Sans battle itself, not replaying earlier Genocide content. Another Steam guide notes a user taking 50 hours to beat Sans, again indicating many direct retries of the fight. Nowhere does the evidence say you must replay a large part of the route after each death, and the descriptions of the Sans fight treat it like a standard boss with repeated attempts. Therefore, the claim that you must redo a significant portion of the route before attempting Sans again is contradicted by the evidence.

Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly recommend preparing for the Sans fight by bringing strong healing items to improve survivability. The Steam guide 'Beating Sans' has a detailed 'Gearing Up' section that emphasizes the importance of having needed items during the Sans fight and lists high-heal options like Butterscotch Pie (full heal), Instant Noodles (90 HP), Mettaton Face Steak (60 HP), Snow Pieces (45 HP), Sea Tea (for speed plus some healing), and Legendary Hero (40 HP). It notes that while not strictly required, these items make the fight easier and that one should fill remaining inventory slots with Legendary Heroes. The Undertale Amino ‘How to Defeat Sans Guide’ similarly stresses armor doesn’t matter but recommends Burnt Pan for extra healing from consumables and suggests high-heal food such as Butterscotch Pie, Instant Noodles, Face Steak, Snowman Pieces, and Legendary Heroes. None of the evidence contradicts the claim that stocking up on and prioritizing high-heal items increases the player’s chances of surviving Sans’s long attack sequences; instead, it directly supports this strategy.

The evidence repeatedly describes using high-heal items (like Butterscotch Pie, Instant Noodles, Face Steak, Legendary Heroes, etc.) during the Sans fight to recover large amounts of HP. The Steam guide 'Beating Sans' explicitly recommends the Butterscotch Pie as healing all HP and discusses timing heals around Sans's attacks, and notes that healing items are important in this battle. None of the sources say that such items cannot be used, that they heal less than usual, or that there are no safe windows; instead they explain that Karma (KR) makes healing trickier but still usable. There is no explicit contradiction of the claim that using Butterscotch Pie or similar high-heal items during safe windows when HP is low allows substantial health restoration.

The evidence discusses recommended items for the Sans fight on the Genocide route, listing Snow Pieces (Snowman Pieces) as healing 45 HP and describing general healing strategies, but it does not mention Tough Glove as a healing item at all (it is a weapon elsewhere in Undertale guides) nor does it explicitly characterize Snowman Pieces or any other items as specifically ‘mid-tier’ or ‘suitable for use in the earlier parts of the battle when the player has taken cumulative Karma damage.’ However, there is no explicit statement in the evidence that contradicts the claim that Snowman Piece or other mid-tier healing items can be suitably used early in the fight under Karma damage. Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly note that healing during the Sans fight is risky and that using healing items can prolong the fight or be ineffective if you cannot survive the following attacks. For example, the Steam guide 'HOW TO BEAT THE BOSSES' states in the Sans section: 'I should state that eating food prolongs the fight unless it is on the turn where he tries to spare you.' This supports the idea that healing must be timed carefully relative to upcoming attacks. The 'Beating Sans' guide also emphasizes how Sans deals continuous damage with no invincibility frames and has Karma (KR) that keeps draining HP, which makes small heals comparatively less useful if you are likely to be hit again immediately. None of the evidence contradicts the claim that very small healing items are generally a poor choice unless the player is confident they will survive the next attack volley; rather, the evidence is consistent with the need to use healing judiciously and the high risk of wasting it. Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly state that attack/defense stats and equipment do not matter in the Sans fight, because Sans has 1 DEF and dodges every attack until the scripted final hit. The evidence repeatedly emphasizes that in the Sans fight, traditional offensive stats and boosts are largely irrelevant, while survival depends on dodging skill and appropriate healing/utility items. Multiple guides in the evidence describe the Sans boss fight in the Genocide route and show that the encounter begins with Sans talking (monologue/dialogue) before the player actually starts dodging attacks. Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly describe Sans's behavior after being hit in the Genocide fight. The evidence describes Sans’s opening attack in detail: he immediately slams you down, raises bone spears from the bottom, throws bone waves, and then uses four waves of Gaster Blasters. Specifically, the Steam guide’s Part 1 list says the first attack (“Strongest Attack”) is: “Slams you downward, raises bone spears from the bottom, throws a wave of bones at you, and sends four waves of blasters at you,” and the other guide similarly notes the strongest attack at the start. The evidence describes Sans's fight and mentions blue bones and the blue soul (gravity) mechanic, but never states that blue bones appear *when* the soul turns blue or that blue bones are specifically tied to that state. Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly describe Sans’s bone attacks as coming in fixed, learnable patterns that can be dodged with small, careful movements, and they emphasize that execution/timing is strict. The evidence describes Gaster Blaster attacks in the Sans fight and repeatedly advises moving away from where the blasters are aiming to dodge them. Don't stop half way and change your mind while your moving. Once you pick a direction, fully commit to it." These descriptions are consistent with the idea that moving quickly away (which can be diagonally or vertically, depending on the blaster’s aim) allows the player to evade the laser. The evidence describes Sans’s Genocide-route fight having an initial organized phase followed by a second phase where attacks are randomized, faster, and more complex. The Steam guide’s Part 2 notes that after the intermission Sans uses “five randomized attacks that are different each time,” including Clips (rapid teleports through various combined attacks), Meta Bones in the menu, more chaotic Gaster Blasters, and complex patterns like Slam and “Be The Leaf,” and a ‘Last Attack’ that mixes many prior attacks with additional Gaster Blasters and wall bones. The Undertale Amino guide similarly describes Part 2 attacks (Clips A/B, Meta Bones, Guided GasterBlasters, Slam, Be The Leaf, Final Attack) as faster and more difficult, with mixed bone waves, multiple attack types chained together, and heavy Gaster Blaster usage. The evidence explains Sans’s special damage effect, Karma Retribution (KR), in detail. It also highlights that bones can appear on the bottom of the menu during your turn, and that meta bones (bone attacks in the menu) occur during your turn in the second phase and can reduce you to 1 HP ("These don't count as attacks, as they happen during your turn, and they can never drop you below 1 HP."). Another guide states that eating food actually prolongs the fight unless it's during the special-attack turn, not that there is a safe healing window ("I should state that eating food prolongs the fight unless it is on the turn where he tries to spare you."). Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly describe that healing during Sans's more complex/busy attacks is dangerous because of constant damage and KR. The evidence describes many specific tips and patterns for Sans’s attacks, but it does not address or contradict the strategic advice that a player should focus on anticipating only the next one or two incoming bones rather than entire long sequences. Multiple guides in the evidence describe Sans’s final attack in the Genocide fight as a long, multi-part sequence that mixes fast bones, gravity changes, Gaster Blasters, and tight, shifting gaps. The Undertale Amino guide’s “Final Attack” section likewise details a multi-component pattern: multiple Slams, Be The Leaf (opposite-moving bone rows), Bone Waves, bone blocks to weave through, a bone funnel, wall jumps, spinning laser focus (from Gaster Blasters), and 36 slams that deal 1 damage each. Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly describe the Sans fight on the Genocide route as extremely difficult and requiring many attempts, emphasizing practice and learning. The evidence repeatedly describes Sans’s attacks as including various patterns of Gaster Blasters and complex bone waves, and the guides emphasize learning how these patterns work and how to dodge them (e.g., the Steam guide’s detailed sections on “Gaster Blasters v1/v2,” “Clips,” and overlapping bone attacks, and the Amino guide’s descriptions of ‘Guided GasterBlasters,’ ‘Bone Wave,’ and the final attack sequence). There is no explicit contradiction in the evidence to the statement that learning the timing of Gaster Blaster attacks and overlapping bone waves is essential for consistent survival.

Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly emphasize that timing your healing during Sans's fight is important because of his constant, rapid attacks and the KR (Karma) effect. For example, the Steam guide 'Beating Sans' notes that KR makes it hard to use healing items at certain times and recommends arranging inventory so you can quickly heal during brief lulls, and warns that eating food prolongs the fight unless done on specific turns. This supports the idea that using healing items only in the smallest safe windows is critical for managing resources and surviving the long battle. No evidence contradicts this description.

Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly recommend learning Sans's attacks as patterns and in ordered chunks, which supports the description. The Steam guide 'Beating Sans' states that in Part 1 'his first 13 attacks come in the same order' and lists them, calling this 'The Organized Part' and noting 'after 13, I can't help you. But, after you get through all of Sans' dialogue, you should have just survived Pocket bones if you didn't heal. Then just attack again.' This encourages memorizing the fixed sequence of attacks. The Undertale Amino 'How to Defeat Sans Guide' similarly breaks the fight into 'Attacks Part 1 (First half)' and 'Attacks Part 2 (Second half)', with detailed descriptions and an 'Order of attacks' list for the first 13 moves, again treating the fight as composed of repeatable patterns and segments. The IGN boss guide also describes Sans's fight in terms of recurring attack types (bones, platforms, skull beams, special attack) that you learn to dodge. Nowhere does the evidence claim that such pattern memorization or chunking is ineffective; instead, the structure of the guides themselves embodies and recommends this strategy. Therefore, the description that memorizing and isolating attack patterns and mentally building the encounter in chunks is an effective strategy is consistent with, and supported by, the evidence.

Multiple guides in the evidence explicitly describe the Sans fight in the Genocide route as a survival-focused battle where Sans dodges all attacks until the scripted end, making damage output largely irrelevant and emphasizing endurance instead. The Steam boss guide states that Sans will "dodge all of yours" attacks and that his fight is a "punishment" with quick, unforgiving attacks, focusing on dodging and surviving rather than dealing damage. The IGN boss guide similarly notes that Sans "will dodge all of yours" attacks and details only survival tactics (dodging bones, platforms, blasters, and waiting out his special attack) as the means to progress. None of the evidence contradicts the claim that patience and survival-oriented play are more important than aggressive damage output; instead, they strongly support it.

The flowchart describes a general procedure to become President in BitLife: start with a smart/good‑looking character in a presidential country, focus on education, study Political Science at university, then pursue graduate education (Law or Business) or leadership careers, maintain good health and a clean record, build wealth, enter local politics (School Board, Mayor), climb to higher offices (Governor, Congress/Senate), then run for President and, if necessary, run again while maintaining approval and avoiding scandals. The evidence from wikiHow and Game Rant confirms these points:

- Starting character: Both sources state you should start in the United States (a presidential system) with high looks and smarts. (wikiHow: “Make sure you start as a character with high looks and smarts born in the United States.” Game Rant: “start with a character that has looks and brains… make sure your character lives in the United States.”)
- Education: Both say to go to university for Political Science and then to Law School or Business School. (wikiHow: “Enroll in Political Science… After finishing a Political Science degree, go to Business or Law school…” Game Rant: “opt for Political Sciences… after graduation, you can either opt for Law School or Business School.”)
- Careers and wealth: Both recommend working (especially as a lawyer or business roles) to earn a lot of money for campaigns. (wikiHow: “Getting a job as a lawyer… will also help generate a lot of money.” Game Rant: “Business school can help you get funding for campaigns… law school can help you advance politically… you should try earning and saving a lot of money.”)
- Reputation/behavior: wikiHow emphasizes not getting in trouble with the law and maintaining high approval; Game Rant similarly stresses running good campaigns and leveraging looks and social media. This aligns with the flowchart’s “maintain good health and reputation” and “avoid illegal activities.”
- Social media/popularity: Both sources say to join social media and post often to gain followers, matching the flowchart’s emphasis on popularity and leadership, though the chart doesn’t go into that detail; it is not contradictory.
- Political ladder: wikiHow: “You can start by running for the School Board or for Mayor… After a few terms as Mayor, run for Governor.” Game Rant: “try gigs that showcase leadership, like becoming a school board director… you can also run for Senate or Mayor… As you pursue these roles and climb the political ladder…” This matches the flowchart’s “local office (School Board Director, Mayor)” and “higher offices (Governor, Congress).” Congress/Senate is an additional but consistent example; there is no evidence saying you cannot go through Congress.
- Age and timing: Both sources say to work until at least age 35 before running for President, consistent with the flowchart’s implicit progression through career and politics before a presidential run.
- Running for President and retrying: wikiHow: “Run for President once you've got at least $80 million… If you don't get elected, you might be able to try again if your character isn't very old.” Game Rant: “When you have gathered around $80 million, you can begin campaigning… If you end up losing the campaign, you can always give it another shot.” This aligns with the flowchart’s “Run for President” and “Repeat if Necessary… run again next election.”
- Managing the presidency: While the sources don’t deeply detail in‑office management, they stress maintaining approval and avoiding scandals during political careers, which is consistent with “keep approval high, avoid scandals and impeachment.” No evidence contradicts that this is how to succeed as President in BitLife.

None of the cited documents contain statements that explicitly contradict any step or dependency in the flowchart. Differences are only in level of detail (e.g., specific money amounts, Bitizenship requirement, or social media mechanics), which the flowchart simply omits but does not misstate. Therefore, the flowchart is consistent with the provided evidence.

The flowchart describes a general money-making progression in Stardew Valley: early crops (parsnips, potatoes, strawberries), then fishing, foraging, mining, animal products, profitable seasonal crops (blueberries in summer, cranberries in fall), artisan goods, winter preparation/foraging, and finally investing in upgrades and infrastructure, with quests and festivals as side income. The provided evidence contains multiple guides and forum posts that are consistent with this structure:

- Early crops and farming for money are supported. The money-making thread states: “Farming is incredibly lucrative. End game, turning those farmed goods into wine/pickles is even more lucrative, but even just selling fruits and veggies outright is great money!” and specifically recommends high-profit crops like cauliflower and multi-harvest crops such as strawberries, blueberries, and cranberries ("focus on high-profit crops like cauliflower until you get stuff that gives multiple crops per planting -- strawberries, blueberries, and cranberries"). This aligns with the flowchart’s Spring crop focus and later seasonal crops.
- Fishing as a strong early-game money source is repeatedly endorsed. The minmaxing guide says: “Fishing is amazing in the very early game… worth considering ditching farming entirely, and instead investing all your time and resources into fishing.” Another thread notes: “And don't discredit how far fishing can get you! … lots of people recommend fishing as the ideal early money maker.” This matches the flowchart’s early move from crops to fishing for gold.
- Foraging as income and support is also supported: turning forageables into seeds for profit is recommended (“Turning forageables into seasonal seeds and then selling those seeds earns a nice chunk of change”), and salmonberries/blackberries are highlighted as important seasonal forage for energy and some value. The flowchart’s inclusion of foraging as a step is consistent with this.
- Mining and metal bars as important progression are explicitly recommended: “Similarly, mining should be a priority as soon as you can enter the mines. Metal bars are important in making progress with upgrades and crafting.” This matches the flowchart’s mining step yielding ores and bars.
- Animal products and buildings as a money source are confirmed. One guide says: “Barns and coops with animals are a large investment output, but end up making a lot of money fairly quickly once you have them. Making mayo is one of my go-to early game moneymakers.” Another player notes focusing on pigs and later ancient fruit wine, showing animals as a major income path. This aligns with the flowchart’s coop/barn step and “steady income from eggs, milk, etc.”
- The specific seasonal crops in the chart are supported: blueberries as a strong Summer crop (“live the watering free dehydrator high life of 200+ blueberries × 4 harvests… blueberries typically sell for 50 Each”) and cranberries as a key Fall multi-harvest crop (“focus on high-profit crops… strawberries, blueberries, and cranberries”).
- Artisan goods being more lucrative than raw products is repeatedly stated: “End game, turning those farmed goods into wine/pickles is even more lucrative” and “Focus on making and selling ancient fruit wine, it gives an awesome cash… It's wine is the most expensive and if you have a cellar where you can age the wine it will give a good money.” Another guide emphasizes mayo and cheese as better than selling raw animal products. This matches the flowchart’s artisan goods step and “high-value processed goods.”
- Winter as a time for preparation and foraging is consistent with the Steam guide: “Winter is the only season crops won't grow (outside).” That same guide suggests using winter for tool upgrades and other non-farming tasks, which fits the flowchart’s “Winter: Preparation and Foraging” and “upgraded tools, cleared land, extra resources.”
- Investing in upgrades and infrastructure (tools, sprinklers, barns/coops, etc.) is strongly recommended across the minmaxing guide and tips (e.g., “Speaking of upgrades: get them ASAP, there's no point in waiting, and it's the best investement you can do.”). This matches the flowchart’s final investment step.
- Quests and festivals as additional income are also supported: “Doing errands from the help wanted board is another way to make a bit of money” and festivals like the Egg Festival are discussed as important for buying profitable seeds (strawberries), which indirectly support money-making. Including them as part of the strategy is consistent.

No part of the flowchart asserts a concrete numerical claim or sequence that is explicitly contradicted by the evidence. The evidence offers alternative optimizations (e.g., some players fish more and farm less in early Spring, or rush ancient fruit instead of pigs), but none of these explicitly deny that the flowchart’s steps are valid or possible. Therefore, there is no explicit contradiction, and under the given rules the flowchart should be judged true.

The evidence describes multiple early-game money-making strategies in Stardew Valley, including one guide that explicitly recommends *selling* the free starting Parsnip seeds on Day 1 instead of planting them, but it does not claim that planting them is impossible or that they do not generate income. The evidence discusses many early-game money-making strategies (fishing, mining, tea saplings, high-profit crops like cauliflower, strawberries, blueberries, cranberries) but does not mention potatoes at all. The Stardew Valley Wiki entry for Potato explicitly states: "When harvested, each Potato plant gives 1 potato, with a random chance to produce extra potatoes, partially based on Daily Luck." The Stardew Valley Wiki states that Strawberry Seeds are purchased at the Egg Festival for 100g and that mature plants keep producing strawberries every 4 days after an 8‑day growth period, making them a strong profit crop for Spring. Forum posts also describe strawberries as a high-profit crop and note that seeds are only sold at the Egg Festival, encouraging players to save money to buy many seeds there. Nothing in the evidence contradicts the claim that buying as many Strawberry Seeds as you can at the Egg Festival secures one of the most lucrative Spring crops; instead, the evidence supports it.

The Strawberry Seeds wiki states they are a spring crop that takes 8 days to mature and then produces a new harvest every 4 days. The Egg Festival (when you can buy the seeds) occurs on Spring 13. Planting immediately on the 13th yields a first harvest on the 21st and a second on the 25th; there is not enough time for an additional 4-day regrowth cycle before Spring ends on the 28th. This is the maximum possible number of harvests in that first year, and no evidence suggests a way to get more Strawberry harvests in Spring if you delay planting. Therefore, planting immediately after the festival does indeed maximize the number of Strawberry harvests before Spring ends.

The Stardew Valley Wiki entry for Strawberry Seeds states that after the initial 8-day growth period, Strawberry plants have a regrowth time of 4 days and produce 1 Strawberry each harvest, with a small chance for extra Strawberries. This confirms that Strawberry plants do regrow and produce new Strawberries every 4 days after the first harvest, enabling repeated profit from a single planting.

The evidence describes fishing as a strong early-game money-making method.
