The flowchart’s steps match the procedures described in the evidence and are not contradicted by any source.

Step A: "Acquire an Incantation" – Evidence states you must have the incantation in your inventory, obtained from NPCs, prayerbooks, or loot (wikiHow: “Make sure the incantation you want to use is in your inventory. You can buy them from NPCs, find them in prayer books, or loot them from enemies!”). This is consistent.

Step B: "Check Required Stats" – Both guides emphasize Faith (and Mind) requirements. SteelSeries: “Pro Tip: Ensure you have sufficient Faith to use the incantation.” wikiHow: “Many spells have requirements that prevent classes not predisposed to magic from casting incants.” No contradiction.

Step C: "Equip a Sacred Seal" – SteelSeries: “In order to cast any spell or incantation, you must have a sacred seal equipped.” wikiHow: “Equip a Sacred Seal… Only Prophet and Confessor classes have a Sacred Seal when they start the game.” This matches.

Step D: "Add the Incantation to Your Spell Slots at a Site of Grace" – SteelSeries: “Before you can cast any incantation, you need to equip it to one of your five memory slots. This is done at a Site of Grace: Rest… Select ‘Memorize Spells.’ Choose the incantations you want to add…” wikiHow: “Equip them with your memory slots… At Sites of Lost Grace, you’ll be able to equip your incants.” This is exactly what the node describes.

Step E: "Equip the Sacred Seal in Main Hand or Off-hand" – SteelSeries: “Open your equipment menu and assign the Sacred Seal to either your left or right hand.” wikiHow: “Equip a Sacred Seal… Once you have a Sacred Seal equipped, you can use incants in battle!” The ordering (memorize then equip, or equip then memorize) is not fixed in the evidence, and nothing states that the flowchart’s order is impossible or wrong.

Step F: "Select the Incantation" – SteelSeries: “use the D-Pad (up button) to cycle through your memorized spells until you select the desired incantation.” wikiHow: “Select the incantation on your screen (using the arrow keys on your keyboard or D-pad button on your remote).” This matches.

Step G: "Cast the Incantation" – SteelSeries: “Press the light attack button (R1 for right hand or L1 for left hand, depending on where your seal is equipped) to cast the selected incantation.” wikiHow: “Press the Attack button to cast the incantation… click the left mouse button / press the right bumper / hit R1 to cast it.” The flowchart’s requirement of a Sacred Seal, selected incantation, and FP is consistent with these descriptions; both sources note FP (mana) and stat requirements implicitly or explicitly.

Step H: "Manage Your FP and Stamina (use Flask of Cerulean Tears if needed)" – While the evidence doesn’t spell out stamina management in detail, it clearly treats incantations as consuming FP (Mind stat, mana pool) and implies the need to manage that resource (wikiHow: “Mind should be at level 40, to increase your Mana pool.”). Using the Flask of Cerulean Tears to restore FP is standard in-game behavior and is not contradicted anywhere in the provided documents.

Step I: "Experiment and Adapt with Different Incantations" – Both guides encourage choosing different incantations based on range, effects, and build: SteelSeries describes many types of incantations and different seals with specific bonuses; wikiHow: “Incantations have varying ranges and effects, so take that into mind…” and discusses different Seals’ pros and cons. Experimentation and adaptation are consistent with this advice.

No step in the flowchart asserts anything that is explicitly denied by the evidence. Differences in emphasis (e.g., the flowchart explicitly mentioning stamina) are additive, not contradictory. Therefore, the flowchart is consistent with the facts in the sources.

The evidence explains that in Elden Ring, incantations are magical spells that can be obtained in multiple ways: they can be purchased from various NPC trainers/merchants (Brother Corhyn, Miriel, D, Gowry, Gurranq, etc.), looted from the world (e.g., from prayerbooks, chests, or specific locations), or received as rewards from bosses or mini‑bosses (e.g., Dragon Heart from bosses traded for dragon incantations, Erdtree Avatar drops specific incantations, Dragonkin Soldier drops Frozen Lightning Spear, Putrid Avatar and others drop flask-related and offensive incantations). The guides then describe equipping and casting these incantations via Sacred Seals. Nothing in the evidence contradicts the statement that acquiring an incantation as loot, as a boss reward, or from merchants/teachers gives the player a magical spell that can later be cast. This is fully consistent with the mechanics described.

The evidence repeatedly describes incantations in Elden Ring as a type of magic that can be purchased from NPC trainers, found as loot, or obtained via items like prayerbooks, and then equipped and cast by the player for healing, curing status effects, dealing damage, and other support or offensive purposes. This matches the target description that incantations are a category of magical spells the player can find or purchase and then use in combat or support roles. There is no statement in the evidence contradicting this description.

The evidence explains how to use incantations and notes that spells have stat requirements (e.g., Faith, sometimes Mind) and that you cannot cast an incantation if you do not meet those requirements, which is indicated by an 'x' on the spell icon. However, nowhere in the provided documents does it state that the attribute requirements are explicitly listed per incantation in a way that a player can check them, nor does it mention checking both Faith and Intelligence requirements from such a list. Since the claim about verifying requirements by checking the attribute requirements listed for each incantation is not confirmed and is not explicitly contradicted by the evidence, and the task requires an explicit contradiction to judge the statement false, the description cannot be judged false based on the given evidence.

The evidence explains that incantations in Elden Ring are spells that have stat requirements, typically in Faith and sometimes in other stats like Intelligence. It notes that spells have requirements that can prevent classes not predisposed to magic from casting them, and that if you don’t meet those requirements an ‘x’ appears on the spell’s icon and you can’t cast it. It also states that anyone can level any stat, and recommends leveling Faith (and Mind) to use incantations effectively. This directly supports the idea that once the player increases Faith (and Intelligence, when needed) to meet an incantation’s listed requirements, that incantation becomes usable/castable by that character. No part of the evidence contradicts this behavior.

The evidence repeatedly states that Sacred Seals are the tools used to cast incantations. The evidence explicitly states that Sacred Seals are a type of weapon used to cast incantations: 'Sacred Seals are a type of Weapon in Elden Ring. The evidence explains that to use incantations in Elden Ring, you must rest at a Site of Grace and select the 'Memorize Spells' option, then choose the incantations you want to add to your memory slots. This matches the description that interacting with a Site of Grace and selecting 'Memorize Spells' lets a player memorize an incantation so it can be assigned to spell slots. The evidence explains that to use incantations you must first equip them in your memory slots via the 'Memorize Spells' option at a Site of Grace. Multiple evidence sources explicitly state that to cast incantations you must equip a Sacred Seal in either hand. The evidence documents repeatedly state that to use incantations in Elden Ring, the player must first equip them and then use the D-pad (or keyboard arrow keys on PC) to cycle through memorized spells and select the desired incantation. The evidence describes how to use incantations in Elden Ring, including equipping a sacred seal, memorizing incantations, selecting them, and pressing the attack button. The evidence describes Elden Ring mechanics, including flasks (Crimson and Cerulean), FP, incantations, and how to use them, but does not anywhere state or imply that the Flask of Cerulean Tears replenishes FP when used with a low or empty FP bar. The evidence describes how incantations in Elden Ring work: they are Faith-based spells with varied effects including healing, curing status, dealing damage, and enchanting/buffing. The flowchart’s steps match the documented procedures and do not conflict with the evidence.

1. Creating a developer account and organization:
- Evidence 1 (AIXR) states you must register as a developer on the Oculus/Meta developer website and create an organization: “Go to the Oculus developer website and log in to your existing meta account… Once verified… ‘Create a New Organization’… Enter a name for the developer account… accept the terms and conditions and click submit… Congratulations you now have a developer account!”
- Evidence 2 (VR Expert) similarly says: “Step 1: Register as a Developer… Enter your developer organization’s name… You are now registered as a developer.”
This is consistent with node A: “Create a Developer Account on the Oculus Developer website.”

2. Installing the Oculus app and pairing the headset:
- Evidence 1: “Once you’ve done that, you can enable developer mode from the Oculus app on your phone. Just go to Settings, then Devices, and select your Quest 2.” It presupposes the Oculus app is installed and the headset is associated with it.
- Evidence 3 (Varwin docs) explicitly describes installing the Oculus mobile app and pairing the Quest 2: “Download the Oculus mobile app on your Android or iOS phone and log in… Click on the Devices tab at the bottom of the screen and link to the new Oculus Quest 2 headset… Wait until the pairing is established.”
This supports nodes B and C: installing the app and pairing the Quest 2 with the app.

3. Enabling Developer Mode in the app:
- Evidence 1: “Once you’ve done that, you can enable developer mode from the Oculus app on your phone. Just go to Settings, then Devices, and select your Quest 2. Under the device settings, you’ll see an option to enable developer mode.”
- Evidence 2: “Enable Developer Mode with the Oculus App… On your phone, open the Oculus application… Devices… After selecting your device, scroll down and click on Developer Mode. Tap the toggle switch to activate developer mode.”
- Evidence 3: “Go to the devices tab and select the newly paired headset. From the list of headset settings, select Developer mode… Switch Developer Mode to On (blue) using the slider on the right.”
These all align with node D: “Enable Developer Mode in the Oculus app.”

4. Restarting the headset after enabling Developer Mode:
- None of the evidence documents explicitly say that restarting the headset is required after toggling Developer Mode. However, they also do not state that restarting is unnecessary or forbidden. Evidence 1 and 2 simply end with developer mode being enabled; Evidence 3 shows that after enabling developer mode, the user continues to use the headset without mentioning a restart requirement.
- The guideline for judgment requires an explicit contradiction in the evidence to mark the flowchart as false. There is no statement like “Do not restart” or “Restarting is not needed and will disable developer mode.” Therefore, including a restart step (node E) is not contradicted by the evidence; it is at worst an extra precautionary step.

5. Verifying Developer Mode via Developer Hub / ADB:
- Evidence 2 describes using the Meta Quest Developer Hub (MQDH) to enable and manage Developer Mode, which also implies it can be used to verify that Developer Mode is active: “Enable Developer Mode with the Meta Quest Developer Hub… Click on the toggle switch next to Developer Mode… Click Next and then Finish to active developer mode your Quest 2 or Quest Pro.” MQDH shows device status, so using it to verify the mode is reasonable.
- Evidence 1 describes allowing USB debugging and installing Oculus ADB drivers to push builds from a computer after enabling developer mode: “Using the USB cable… connect it to your computer… select ‘Allow USB debugging’… install the Oculus ADB Drivers… Now you can directly push builds from your computer to your headset.” This is consistent with verifying that developer mode/USB debugging is functioning via ADB.
Node F (“Verify Developer Mode is Enabled via Oculus Developer Hub / ADB”) is therefore consistent with the documented tooling and behavior.

Because no step in the flowchart is explicitly contradicted by the provided sources, and several steps are directly supported, the flowchart should be judged true under the given criteria.

Multiple documents describe starting this process by visiting the Oculus developer website. The flowchart contains at least one element that is explicitly inconsistent with the evidence.

1. Incorrect Monkey Knowledge name and requirement
- Flowchart node F says: "Ensure Monkey Knowledge 'There's No Sun God Like A Vengeful One' is unlocked" and uses that as the prerequisite.
- The evidence states the required Monkey Knowledge is "There Can Be Only One" in the Magic section:
  - Bloons Wiki Vengeful True Sun God page: "The Vengeful True Sun God can only be created by (1) enabling the There Can Be Only One MK..." and in Troubleshooting: "Ensure that: The There Can Be Only One MK in the Magic section has been enabled."
  - Blooncyclopedia VTSG page: "There Can Be Only One must be unlocked and enabled. (This means the VTSG cannot be summoned in CHIMPS or if Monkey Knowledge is disabled through any other means.)"
- There is no mention anywhere in the evidence of a Monkey Knowledge called "There's No Sun God Like A Vengeful One". Therefore, the flowchart's prerequisite MK name is factually wrong and contradicts the documented requirement.

2. Order and placement of Anti-Bloon and Legend of the Night
- The flowchart step D says to "place & fully upgrade Legend of the Night ... and Anti-Bloon ... before upgrading to True Sun God" and connects from C (the step that already upgrades to True Sun God). This implies placing them after the Sun Temple has already been upgraded to True Sun God, which conflicts with the documented procedure.
- Evidence specifies they must already be present outside the Sun Temple's range *before* the Sun Temple is upgraded to True Sun God:
  - Bloons Wiki: "Outside of the Temple's range, place and upgrade The Anti-Bloon ... and the Legend of The Night ...; they cannot be sacrificed to the creation of the TSG, otherwise the upgrade to VTSG will fail. Upgrade the Sun Temple into True Sun God. Upon upgrading, the game will first animate the creation of the True Sun God and then absorb the Anti-Bloon and Legend of the Night..."
  - Blooncyclopedia: "The Anti-Bloon and Legend of the Night must be placed somewhere on the map outside the range of the Sun Temple the player is upgrading." and then: "Upgrade the Sun Temple to a True Sun God... then ... transform into the VTSG."
- The flowchart’s structure and wording make the placement of Anti-Bloon and Legend of the Night occur after the Sun Temple has already been upgraded to True Sun God, which contradicts the explicit requirement that they be present before that upgrade.

Because of these explicit contradictions (wrong MK name and incorrect timing/ordering of placing Anti-Bloon and Legend of the Night relative to the True Sun God upgrade), the flowchart is not consistent with the facts in the provided evidence.

The evidence explains that the Vengeful True Sun God (VTSG) is obtained by first building a Sun Temple and then upgrading it to a True Sun God under specific sacrifice and Monkey Knowledge conditions. It also shows the Super Monkey’s Path 1 upgrade chain in BTD6 as: Sun Avatar → Sun Temple → True Sun God. Therefore, upgrading a placed Super Monkey to Sun Avatar is indeed the first step on that path and provides the base tower needed to continue upgrading toward Sun Temple and eventually VTSG. No statement in the evidence contradicts this; it is consistent with the described upgrade progression.

The evidence explains that to create a Vengeful True Sun God (and, by implication, a qualifying Sun Temple on the way), the Sun Temple must have max sacrifices in three of the four categories (Primary, Military, Magic, Support), and later the True Sun God must have max sacrifices in all four categories. Specifically, the Bloons Wiki page states: "Build a Sun Temple with max sacrifices ($50,000 spent) in 3 out of 4 tower categories" and the Blooncyclopedia page reiterates that the Sun Temple "must have maxed-out sacrifices in three tower categories". The target description claims that sacrificing $50,000 from Primary, Military, and Magic when upgrading Sun Avatar to Sun Temple "creates a Sun Temple that qualifies for later transformation into the True Sun God". This is consistent with the requirement of any three categories; Support is not mandatory at the Sun Temple stage. No part of the evidence contradicts this; it only adds that later, for the True Sun God, all four categories must be maxed. Therefore, the description is factually accurate with respect to the provided evidence.

The target description is a piece of general strategic advice: it claims that in Bloons TD 6, having a lot of money and enough open space before attempting the Vengeful True Sun God (VTSG) process helps avoid running out of resources or placement area during sacrifices and upgrades. The provided evidence explains in detail that creating a VTSG is extremely expensive (over $1.2M+ depending on difficulty) and requires many towers for sacrifices (at least $350,000 total in sacrifices plus three tier‑5 Super Monkeys, and a minimum of 7 towers on screen at once). It also notes that the process is complex and that you must place The Anti‑Bloon and Legend of the Night outside the Sun Temple’s range and arrange multiple sets of sacrificial towers. All of this is consistent with the idea that having ample money and map space beforehand is prudent to avoid running out of cash or room mid‑process. Nowhere does the evidence state or imply that this advice is wrong or impossible; there is no explicit contradiction such as “having extra money/space does not help” or similar. Since the judgment rules require an explicit denial to mark the statement false, and none is present, the description must be considered true.

The evidence explains that to create a Vengeful True Sun God (and by extension a fully buffed True Sun God), you must sacrifice at least $50,000 worth of towers in each relevant category, including Support, to both the Sun Temple and then the True Sun God. It explicitly describes placing and upgrading towers inside the Sun Temple's radius so their total cost in each category reaches the required threshold, and notes that Support sacrifices give discount buffs. This matches the description that you can place support towers around the Sun Temple to prepare the necessary Support category value for later upgrades and buffs. No statement in the evidence contradicts this; instead, it directly supports it.

The evidence explains that for creating a (Vengeful) True Sun God, what matters is the total cash value of towers sacrificed in each category, and that this value is based on their cost (with discounts and difficulty taken into account). It explicitly notes that sacrificing Support towers to a Sun Temple causes discounts, which then reduce the value of later sacrifices, and that players must compensate by using towers and upgrades with higher base cost. This directly implies that upgrading placed support towers to higher tiers increases their cost and thus their sacrificial value toward the $50,000-per-category requirement. There is no statement contradicting this; instead, the mechanics described support it.

The description claims that to obtain a fully powered True Sun God with three sacrifice categories (Primary, Military, Magic) you need at least $50,000 of each of those three types within its range when upgrading Sun Temple to True Sun God. The evidence explains that for the True Sun God, you must sacrifice at least $50,000 worth of towers in all four categories (Primary, Military, Magic, Support): "At least $50,000 worth of towers in all categories (Primary, Military, Magic, Support) to the Tier 5 True Sun God (ie. $200,000 or more total in sacrifices) have been sacrificed." and similarly in the VTSG article: "then must be upgraded to a True Sun God with maxed-out sacrifices in all four tower categories." This explicitly contradicts the idea that only three categories are needed for a fully powered True Sun God. Therefore, the target description is false.

The evidence explains that for both the Sun Temple and the True Sun God, maxed sacrifices in a given tower category (Primary, Military, Magic, Support) are obtained by sacrificing at least $50,000 worth of towers in that category within the temple’s range. It also states that Support sacrifices give the temple (and thus the True Sun God / Vengeful True Sun God) powerful support buffs such as discounts, range, attack speed, damage, pierce, and end-of-round cash. While the article goes into more detail for the Vengeful True Sun God, it explicitly notes that these Support-category sacrifices grant such buffs and that the same sacrifice rules apply to the True Sun God. Nowhere does the evidence contradict the claim that sacrificing at least $50,000 worth of Support towers when upgrading to True Sun God grants the Support Sun God buff; instead, it directly supports that mechanic as part of the temple sacrifice system.

The evidence states that to create the Vengeful True Sun God (VTSG), "Outside of the Temple's range, place and upgrade The Anti-Bloon (x-5-x Super Monkey) and the Legend of The Night (x-x-5 Super Monkey); they cannot be sacrificed to the creation of the TSG, otherwise the upgrade to VTSG will fail." It also reiterates that "The Anti-Bloon and Legend of the Night must be placed somewhere on the map outside the range of the Sun Temple the player is upgrading." The target description says that by placing a Super Monkey and fully upgrading it along the bottom path to Legend of the Night, with the relevant Monkey Knowledge active, before upgrading the Sun Temple, you can satisfy one of the prerequisites. This matches the requirement: Legend of the Night is indeed one of the two tier‑5 Super Monkeys that must be present (outside the temple’s range) prior to upgrading the Sun Temple to True Sun God. No statement in the evidence contradicts this; instead, it directly supports it.

The description claims that in Bloons TD 6 you must upgrade a Super Monkey along the top path to The Anti-Bloon as a prerequisite for creating the Vengeful True Sun God. The evidence repeatedly states that The Anti-Bloon is the middle‑path tier 5 (x‑5‑x) Super Monkey, not a top‑path upgrade. For example, the Vengeful True Sun God page says: “Outside of the Temple's range, place and upgrade The Anti-Bloon (x-5-x Super Monkey) and the Legend of The Night (x-x-5 Super Monkey)” and the Super Monkey upgrade list shows The Anti-Bloon on Path 2, not Path 1. Therefore, saying “top path to the Anti-Bloon” is factually wrong, even though having The Anti-Bloon on the map is indeed a prerequisite.

The evidence explains that in Bloons TD 6 the Vengeful True Sun God (VTSG) is created by upgrading a Sun Temple into a True Sun God under specific conditions: (1) the Anti-Bloon and Legend of the Night must be placed on the map outside the Sun Temple’s range, and (2) the Sun Temple must have maxed sacrifices in three categories, then be upgraded to a True Sun God with maxed sacrifices in all four categories. The Blooncyclopedia page states: “The VTSG is created by upgrading a Sun Temple into a True Sun God under specific conditions: … The Anti-Bloon and Legend of the Night must be placed somewhere on the map outside the range of the Sun Temple … The Sun Temple must have maxed-out sacrifices in three tower categories, then must be upgraded to a True Sun God with maxed-out sacrifices in all four tower categories.” The Bloons Wiki page under “Creating a Vengeful True Sun God” describes the same process. This matches the description’s claim that upgrading the Sun Temple to True Sun God while both Legend of the Night and Anti-Bloon are present on the map and all required sacrifices are met triggers the VTSG transformation. No part of the evidence contradicts this; it only adds further requirements like Monkey Knowledge being enabled, which the description does not deny.

The evidence explains that in Bloons TD 6, if the specific conditions are met (There Can Be Only One enabled, max sacrifices on Sun Temple and then True Sun God, and The Anti-Bloon plus Legend of the Night placed outside the sacrifice radius), then after upgrading the Sun Temple it first becomes a True Sun God and then transforms into the Vengeful True Sun God. The article states that this form has a dark/mechanical visual theme and that "Vengeful True Sun God is just like a normal True Sun God except all its attacks dealing substantially more damage" and that it "employs Vengeful Sun Avatars, Dark Spectres, red Sunblasts and additional attacks which all deal twice as much damage compared to the non-vengeful True Sun God." This directly supports the description that fulfilling all prerequisites and sacrifices and allowing the transformation results in a darker-looking, stronger version of the True Sun God.
